title: Quiz: Future Rounds and Ongoing Standardization

questions.question: Which three KEMs are still under consideration in Round 4?
questions.explanation: BIKE, HQC, and Classic McEliece are the three code-based KEMs still under evaluation in Round 4 after SIKE was broken.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of NIST's September 2023 call for additional signature algorithms?

options.text: To find signatures not based on structured lattices
To find faster implementations of FALCON

questions.explanation: NIST is seeking additional signature schemes that are NOT based on structured lattices to provide mathematical diversity beyond ML-DSA and FN-DSA.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of Classic McEliece over other Round 4 KEMs?
questions.explanation: Classic McEliece dates back to 1978, giving it over 45 years of cryptanalysis - more than any other PQC candidate - providing very high confidence in its security.

questions.question: Why has FN-DSA (FALCON) standardization been delayed?

options.text: Implementation complexity with floating-point and Gaussian sampling

questions.explanation: FALCON uses floating-point arithmetic and Gaussian sampling, which are complex to implement securely without side-channel leakage, causing delays in finalization.

questions.question: What is the key size disadvantage of Classic McEliece?
questions.explanation: Classic McEliece has enormous public keys of approximately 261,120 bytes (~261 KB), making it impractical for many applications despite its security confidence.

questions.question: How do FN-DSA signatures compare to ML-DSA signatures in size?
questions.explanation: FN-DSA signatures are approximately 2-3 times smaller than ML-DSA signatures (e.g., ~666 bytes vs ~2,420 bytes), making FALCON valuable for bandwidth-constrained applications.

questions.question: Organizations should wait for all future standards before beginning PQC migration.
questions.explanation: Organizations should start migration NOW with the current standards (ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA). Additional algorithms enhance options but don't replace current standards. Waiting exposes systems to 'harvest now, decrypt later' attacks.
